title: Amsinckia aurantiaca
intro: Amsinckia aurantiaca, also known as orange fiddleneck, is a flowering plant in the borage family (Boraginaceae) that is native to the southwestern United States and northern Mexico. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which may aid in reducing inflammation, boosting the immune system, and protecting against cell damage. The bioactive constituents of Amsinckia aurantiaca include alkaloids, phenolic acids, and flavonoids, such as amsonic acid, amsonicine, and aurantion, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Amsinckia aurantiaca is often used in herbal preparations, including teas, tinctures, and capsules, which are commonly used to support respiratory health, reduce stress, and promote relaxation.
taxonomy: Amsinckia aurantiaca is a species of flowering plant in the family Boraginaceae. It belongs to the genus Amsinckia, which is native to the western United States and Mexico. The genus Amsinckia is often classified within the subfamily Boraginoideae. Amsinckia aurantiaca is further grouped under the subgenus Amsinckia. This classification places Amsinckia aurantiaca within the broader plant kingdom as a member of the flowering plant group.

side_effects: Amsinckia aurantiaca, also known as orange fiddleneck, is a plant native to the southwestern United States and Mexico. While it is considered non-toxic, ingestion of the plant can cause gastrointestinal upset, including nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. The plant's seeds contain a toxic compound called lycopsamine, which can be toxic to humans and animals if ingested in large quantities. Ingestion of the plant can also lead to skin irritation and allergic reactions in some individuals. Additionally, pregnant women and breastfeeding mothers should avoid handling the plant due to the potential risks of exposure.
